text: Yeah, she's gorgeous. And this is Louis, Louis Parker. And this is [? Jono ?] Parker. That's my whole life. That's why I live because they are here, and I love them dearly. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask Shimon and Leisha-- you obviously have heard your mother's story. She's told you her life. Do you have anything that you wanted to say about her story and her life? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something first, [? Shim? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, she's-- as we've always known, she's an amazing lady, with an amazing strength, and a real-- a matriarch of the family, and we're very proud of her, that's for sure. It's been a-- a very strong influence in-- in my life, anyway. I can't speak for Leisha.

speaker: interviewer
text: She told you about her survival, and how she survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked your mother already why she thought she survived. Why do you think she survived? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: For what-- for what purpose or why-- with what-- what skills she-- well, because of, once again, her strength, her chutzpah. She really-- she's a-- she's a survivor. She's not-- she won't just up and accept things, which makes her difficult to live with sometimes. [LAUGHS] But-- but that-- that's her strength, you know? There's no-- no doubt about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leisha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I agree 100% with-- with what Shim said. And-- and her strength has given us strength. And and I think, this-- no matter what, this will-- her survival, and to-- to show us all how to land on our feet, and how, through hard work, and through thinking things out, that we can achieve what we want to. And that's been, I think, a very important lesson to us all right throughout our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lee, I believe you've done a story on your grandmother's life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, last-- in year six, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I interviewed her so I just learned, you know, how she survived and how, you know, her strength and stuff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: You must be very proud of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- Lee-- Lee was just saying that they are actually doing the at school, and the teacher actually mentioned this project, which is quite amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, you wanted to say something.

text: No, I think I say enough today for one day. [LAUGHS] And I only-- I only say I-- I am happy I have that beautiful family, but I miss my husband very, very much. And [SOBBING] he was the crown of my-- of my head. And I lost him, and I lost my crown. And I'm-- and I'm not the same anymore but I was-- when he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're still a very strong lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to be strong. The children need me. They like me. They love me. Yes. Yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Yes, Lee, is there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The daughter-in-law want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, there is a hanky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we don't always agree on everything, but I think that's two women in a household, it's-- it's very natural, and Liz is a very strong woman, but I learn a lot from  her, too. And I learn a lot about myself through her. And her story is-- is amazing. My family also, my grandparents came from Poland before the war. They lost all their family, and I-- they didn't talk about it as much, their-- what they went through before the war. So [BACKGROUND NOISE] I've learned a lot.

words.text: I learn a lot from  her,

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of my friends had parents who went through the Holocaust, but it wasn't real. I wasn't that connected with it because [? my parents were ?] younger. It's given me a real [BACKGROUND NOISE] [INTERPOSING VOICES] reality to it. So it's not something that just happened. It's part of my family now, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anyone else like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the other thing is-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we had the two extremes in our-- in our family. My father didn't talk about the Holocaust and his family [BACKGROUND NOISE] before the war much at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we know very little about it and it's a project that I want to undertake to find out more about him. But Mother was the other extreme, where she-- she articulated very well and freely. I think my father found it too painful.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very gentle man, and very self-conscious. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I'd like to thank everybody for coming and making the effort to be here with your mother today, and grandmother. Thank you.
